title: Label Encoding: Ordering Categories
concept: Label encoding converts ordered categories into numbers by assigning a sequential value that preserves their relative ranking.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: Why do machine learning algorithms require us to convert categorical features like 'City' into numbers?

options.text: Because they can only perform mathematical calculations on numeric inputs
Because text files take up too much memory
Because categorical features are always errors in the dataset

exercises.explain: Under the hood, ML algorithms are mathematical formulas. They cannot calculate using raw text labels, so everything must become numbers.

cards.title: T-shirt sizes
cards.body: When buying clothes, 'Small', 'Medium', and 'Large' have a natural order. A 'Medium' is larger than a 'Small', and a 'Large' is larger than a 'Medium'.

cards.title: Label Encoding (Ordinal)
cards.body: Label encoding (often called ordinal encoding) maps ordered categories directly to sequential numbers (like 1, 2, 3) to preserve their rank and relative scale.

cards.title: School report cards
cards.body: Think of grades: 'A' gets 4 points, 'B' gets 3, and 'C' gets 2. The GPA system uses label encoding to turn letter ranks into numbers you can calculate.

cards.title: Credit scores
cards.body: If you have a column for credit rating with values 'Poor', 'Fair', and 'Excellent', you can label encode them as 0, 1, and 2. The model learns that 2 is better than 0.

cards.title: Don't use it on unordered data
cards.body: Never use label encoding on unordered categories like 'Apple', 'Banana', and 'Cherry'. Assigning them 1, 2, and 3 tells the model that Cherry is 'three times' or 'greater than' Apple, which is false!

exercises.prompt: Which of these features is a good candidate for label encoding?
exercises.explain: Satisfaction ratings have a clear, natural order, so mapping them to sequential numbers makes sense.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Label encoding is the best way to convert unordered categories like country names (e.g., 'USA', 'France') into numbers.
exercises.explain: False! Using label encoding on unordered data introduces a fake mathematical ranking that will confuse the model.

exercises.sentence: Label encoding preserves the natural ___ of the categories.
exercises.explain: Ordinal/label encoding is specifically designed to keep the ranked relationship between items.

exercises.prompt: Which mapping represents a correct label encoding for educational levels?
exercises.explain: Assigning sequential numbers in ascending order of the degree's rank correctly preserves the education hierarchy.

exercises.prompt: You are modeling restaurant reviews. The feature service_speed contains the labels 'Slow', 'Average', and 'Fast'. You encode them as Slow=0, Average=1, Fast=2. How will a linear model interpret this?

options.text: It will understand that 'Fast' is numerically greater than 'Average', which is greater than 'Slow'
It will assume 'Slow' and 'Fast' are exactly the same
It will ignore the column because the numbers are too small
It will fail to compile because text cannot be converted this way

exercises.explain: Correct! The model treats these encoded numbers as a continuous scale, recognizing the natural ranking. Next up: how we handle categories that have no natural order!
